planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy, seasonal-dry tropical soil in full sun; Kakadu plum is a Northern Territory and Western Australia native tree producing small, round, pale-green fruits with the highest naturally occurring vitamin C concentration of any fruit in the world — approximately 1,000–5,300 mg per 100g (20–50 times more vitamin C than oranges by weight) — and now a globally sought-after ingredient in the native food, cosmetics, and nutraceutical industries.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; Kakadu plum requires excellent drainage and tolerates seasonal drought — water only in establishment first year, then rely on rainfall in tropical monsoonal climate. Terminalia ferdinandiana (Kakadu plum, gubinge, or billygoat plum) is a small to medium-sized deciduous tree native to the open woodland and savanna regions of the Northern Territory and northwestern Western Australia — and the producer of what is recognized as the single richest natural source of vitamin C (ascorbic acid) in the world. The small (1/2–1 inch), round, pale-green to olive-colored fruits contain an extraordinary 1,000–5,300 mg of ascorbic acid per 100g fresh weight — 20 to 80 times more vitamin C than oranges and roughly 100 times more than most commercial fruits. Aboriginal Australians across the Top End and Kimberley have eaten Kakadu plums as a traditional food and medicine for at least 65,000 years. The fruits are also remarkably high in ellagic acid and other polyphenol antioxidants. In contemporary Australian native food and cosmetics industries, Kakadu plum extract has become one of the most sought-after botanical ingredients for antioxidant skincare products, functional foods, and vitamin C supplements — and Kakadu plum jam, sauce, and fruit purée are premium native food products sold across Australia and internationally. The tree adapts well to tropical and subtropical low-humidity conditions with good drainage.
sources: Australian Native Food Industry — Kakadu Plum Factsheet
Journal of Food Chemistry — Ascorbic Acid in Terminalia ferdinandiana

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, loamy soil in part shade; Davidson's plum is a rainforest understory tree from tropical and subtropical Queensland, producing large, dark-purple to almost-black, very tart, sour plums on the trunk and branches (cauliflorous) — the primary fruit of the Australian native food industry for jams, sauces, cheeses, desserts, and gin liqueurs — and remarkably rich in antioxidants and vitamin C.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, loamy soil in part shade; Davidson's plum grows naturally as a rainforest understory tree and requires protection from direct hot afternoon sun. Davidsonia pruriens (Davidson's plum or ooray) is a rainforest understory tree native to the tropical and subtropical rainforests of Queensland, Australia — one of the most commercially important fruits in the Australian native food industry. The tree is notable for its highly unusual growth habit: the large (1.5–2.5 inch), dark-purple to burgundy-black, plum-like fruits grow directly from the trunk and main branches in dense clusters (cauliflory), similar to cacao and jaboticaba. The fruits are intensely, mouth-puckeringly tart — far too sour to eat fresh in quantity without processing — but with extraordinary depth of flavor that has made them the darling of Australian native food chefs and product makers. Davidson's plum jam, Davidson's plum sauce (excellent with game, lamb, and dark meats), Davidson's plum sorbet, Davidson's plum wine and gin, and Davidson's plum dried food products are among the most celebrated products of the native food movement. The fruits are extremely rich in antioxidants — anthocyanins, ellagic acid, vitamin C — with a deep, complex, sour-fruity character that processing and sweetening reveals as intensely flavorful. Hardy to light frost in zone 9, Davidson's plum is increasingly available from Australian specialty nurseries.
sources: Australian Native Food Industry — Davidson's Plum Factsheet
Royal Botanic Garden Sydney — Australian Rainforest Plants

common_name: Bush Lily (Natal Lily, Kaffir Lily, Clivia)

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown clumps in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in shade to part shade; Clivia miniata is one of the finest shade-tolerant ornamental bulb plants in the world — thriving in dense shade under trees where few other ornamentals flower — producing spectacular umbels of 12–20 brilliant orange to yellow, lily-like flowers in spring; the large, strappy evergreen leaves provide year-round structure in the shade garden; CAUTION: All parts are toxic.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in shade to part shade; Clivia dislikes direct hot sun — shade under trees is ideal; allow to become pot-bound for best flowering; CAUTION: All parts are toxic. Clivia miniata (bush lily, Natal lily, or Kaffir lily) is one of the most spectacular and reliable shade-flowering ornamental plants available — a South African native from the shaded forest understory of KwaZulu-Natal and the Eastern Cape that has become a treasured garden plant worldwide. Unlike most ornamental bulbs that demand full sun, clivia thrives in deep shade — exactly the kind of dense shade under large trees where most plants struggle or fail — producing bold, strappy, arching, evergreen leaves (12–18 inches long, dark-green, leathery) that provide attractive year-round structure, and then in late winter to spring, spectacular upright stems bearing umbels of 12–20 large (2–3 inch), tubular, lily-like flowers in brilliant orange with yellow throats, or in cultivated forms, pale-yellow, cream, orange-red, and rare green-flowered varieties. The flowers are followed by clusters of large, round, red to orange berries that persist decoratively for months. Clivia has become one of the most prized and collected ornamental plants in Japan and China, where exceptional yellow and rare variegated-leaf forms command extraordinary prices. All parts of the plant are toxic; do not eat.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Clivia miniata

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring (or early fall) in very well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; Nerine bowdenii is the most cold-hardy nerine species — hardy to zone 7 in well-drained soil — producing spectacular clusters of bright-pink, strap-petaled, spidery flowers on bare stems in autumn (September–November), after most other bulbs have finished; flowers appear before or with the strap-shaped leaves; naturalizes readily in mild climates.

directions: Plant bulbs with the tip just at or slightly above soil level in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; do not disturb once established; water sparingly in summer dormancy. Nerine bowdenii (Cape nerine or Bowden lily) is the most cold-hardy species in the Nerine genus — a South African native bulb from the mountains of the Eastern Cape and KwaZulu-Natal — and one of the most spectacular bulbs for fall flowering in zone 7–10. The plant produces striking, 2-foot bare stems topped with large umbels (6–8 flowers per head) of bright, vivid pink, reflexed, strap-petaled flowers with distinctively wavy, crinkled petal margins — a uniquely spidery, exotic appearance unlike any other fall bulb. The flowers appear in September through November, after the bulb's strappy, strap-shaped leaves have died back for summer — blooming from apparently bare ground, then producing the strap-like leaves after or alongside the flowers. N. bowdenii naturalizes readily into permanent clumps that increase over years and flower more prolifically as they become established. The bulbs bloom best when slightly crowded and resent disturbance. Unlike its tender relative Nerine sarniensis (Guernsey lily), N. bowdenii is hardy in zone 7 with good drainage and a sheltered position. A classic autumn bulb for the Mediterranean-climate and mild-winter garden.

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, loamy soil in part shade; lemon aspen is a Queensland rainforest tree producing small, round, pale-yellow fruits with intensely concentrated, complex lemon-eucalyptus-pine flavor — unrelated to true lemons (Citrus) but with a remarkably bright, complex citrus character prized in Australian native food cuisine for sauces, drinks, dressings, and as a lemon substitute.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, loamy soil in part shade; lemon aspen grows as a rainforest understory tree and performs best with some afternoon shade protection. Acronychia acidula (lemon aspen) is an Australian native rainforest tree from the tropical and subtropical rainforests of northeastern Queensland — one of the most distinctive flavor ingredients in Australian native cuisine and a rising star of the Australian native food industry. The tree produces small (1/2–3/4 inch), round, pale-yellow to white fruits with highly aromatic, intensely concentrated lemon-pine-eucalyptus flavor — a unique, complex, bright citrus character that is entirely different from true lemons (Citrus limon) but provides a distinctively clean, intense, herbal-citrus punch that chefs value highly. The flavor profile is often described as lemon with eucalyptus, thyme, and pine notes — a complexity that normal citrus lacks. Lemon aspen is entirely unrelated to true citrus but has evolved a remarkably similar citrus flavor independently through different chemistry. The fruits are too intense and sharp to eat fresh in quantity but are used in small amounts as flavor accents in dressings, seafood sauces, marinades, desserts, and beverages — both fresh and as preserves, oils, and freeze-dried powder. Commercially produced lemon aspen products are increasingly available in Australian specialty food stores and international gourmet markets.
sources: Australian Native Food Industry — Lemon Aspen

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to part shade; pink kopsia is one of Singapore's most recognizable ornamental shrubs — a member of the Apocynaceae family (related to frangipani and Vinca) producing clusters of pretty, pinwheel-shaped, five-petaled, soft-pink flowers nearly year-round; CAUTION: All parts of the plant contain alkaloids — toxic if ingested.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to part shade; prune lightly after flowering flushes to maintain compact shape; CAUTION: All parts are toxic — do not ingest. Kopsia fruticosa (pink kopsia or Singapore rose) is a compact, tropical evergreen shrub in the Apocynaceae (dogbane) family — the same family as frangipani (Plumeria), vinca (Catharanthus), and allamanda — native to the forests and forest edges of Southeast Asia, particularly the Malay Peninsula, Borneo, and Indonesia. The shrub is an extremely popular and widely planted ornamental in Singapore, Malaysia, and tropical Southeast Asia, where it is one of the most recognizable roadside and garden shrubs. The flowers are similar in shape to periwinkle or frangipani flowers: five-petaled, pinwheel-shaped, 1–1.5 inch across, soft pink to rose-pink with a slightly darker center, produced in small clusters at the branch tips nearly continuously throughout the year in a warm, tropical climate. The effect is of a neat, clean, cheerful, softly-colored shrub covered in pastel-pink flowers year-round. The glossy, elliptic, dark-green leaves provide an attractive backdrop. Like most Apocynaceae, kopsia contains alkaloids (kopsingine and related compounds) that make all plant parts toxic if ingested; do not eat any part of the plant. A must-have for tropical gardens seeking reliable, long-blooming, moderate-sized ornamental shrubs.
sources: Singapore Botanic Gardens — Native and Naturalized Plants of Singapore
University of Malaya — Flora of the Malay Peninsula
Kew Royal Botanic Gardens — Plants of the World Online
